NASA streamed Artemis 2 live in HD on YouTube and NASA TV, but the broadcast had glitches, poor tracking, and missed key moments—unlike SpaceX's slick, multi-camera productions with onboard views. SpaceX's edge comes from reusable rockets, real-time telemetry, and a focus on engaging the public. NASA partners with them on ISS crew flights and future Starship landers, so tech sharing happens where it fits. As for ISS filming the launch: timing and orbit didn't align for a clean view during the short window. Would've been legendary though.

I think Elon is staying silent because he understands the high risks NASA took with Artemis 2.

They launched despite the Orion heat shield showing serious damage and char loss during Artemis 1 — a problem that could cause catastrophic failure during the extreme re-entry. They're also flying during solar maximum, accepting elevated radiation exposure and the real risk of dangerous solar particle events beyond Earth's magnetic field.

Four brave astronauts are basically playing Russian roulette with these known issues, largely due to schedule and political pressure. I really hope they return safely to Earth. Elon probably wouldn't fly a crew on hardware with these potential catastrophe risks.

For this Artemis 2 toilet glitch, the fecal system still worked fine—the fan/controller issue only hit urine venting. So the crew pooped straight into the Universal Waste Management System toilet like normal. It sucks the solids into a bag, compacts it, and they haul it back to Earth.

The CCU (that collapsible urinal in the pics) was just the urine backup, using capillary action to keep things contained in zero-g without floating mess. Saves hauling extra diapers. Solid waste has always been the bigger challenge up there.

Possible reasons for the burning smell on Artemis 2 near the toilet (Universal Waste Management System):

- Heater element or seal overheating (to manage waste in cold microgravity; suspected after recent fan/controller fix).
- Normal operational warmup in the Orion's chilly cabin.
- Minor electrical glitch, like a component getting hot during use.

I'm glad that Artemis 2 launched successfully but there's nothing record-breaking about its mission. It is basically a simplified version of Apollo 8 but 58 years delayed. Neither mission planned a Moon landing but Apollo 8 orbited. Artemis 2's trajectory is a "free return." A rocket burn in Earth orbit injected Artemis 2 into a trans-lunar trajectory that "sling-shots" around the Moon back to Earth without another rocket burn. The fact that the crew is going further from Earth than humans have gone before is sold as an achievement but is just necessary to follow the simple "free return" trajectory. In later missions, Artemis will have to achieve lunar orbit to rendezvous with a lunar lander. So Artemis 2, while safer, is a less complete test than its predecessor, Apollo 8. Despite finally achieving crewed launch, Artemis 2 again demonstrates that today NASA can even replicate the tech achievement of its 1960s Apollo program. NASA's chronically underfunded human space flight has been, for decades, a bloated bureaucracy pushing globslist ideology not US  space tech. It's time to turn US human space flight program over to the Space  Force.

#NASA #ArtemisII    I  completely agree that the Apollo Moon landings were not real. ( And question the "newest adventure ")  However, those of us who question the official narrative must be careful not to undermine our position by sharing fake or fabricated "proofs." Spreading misinformation only hurts the credibility of legitimate skepticism.
For example, claims about WikiLeaks publishing "staged cut scenes" from the Moon landing are false. WikiLeaks has released some unrelated NASA documents in the past — such as old emails discussing the reuse (erasure) of original Apollo videotapes in the 1970s — but nothing resembling leaked "behind-the-scenes" footage of a staged landing. Let's stick to verifiable evidence and avoid anything that can be easily debunked.
Truth-seeking requires intellectual honesty. If we want people to take the inconsistencies in the Apollo story seriously, we need to hold ourselves to a higher standard than those promoting the official version.

Rear-View Mirror

There are many reasons to admire Artemis 2, technology, ambition, the possibility of humans landing on the Moon again. But none of that impressed me as much as one quiet moment: the last images of Earth shrinking in the spacecraft’s rear-view mirror.

At first, it was a planet.
Then a sphere.
Then a dot.

A small, delicate blue dot, like something you might lose between the sofa cushions.

And that’s when the thought arrived, uninvited and slightly embarrassing:
That’s us.

Everything.

Every argument. Every border. Every speech, every war, every carefully constructed reason for why we simply must disagree with each other immediately and loudly, all of it happening on something that, from a short distance away, looks like a misplaced marble.

It raises a difficult question:
Why do we behave as if we’re running a vast empire when, in fact, we’re sharing a dot?

We fight wars that destroy cities, take millions of lives, and leave behind explanations that sound impressive until viewed from space. We damage the climate as if we have a spare planet in storage. We argue about differences that become invisible the moment Earth begins to shrink.

From orbit, there are no lines. No borders. No ideologies. Just a fragile blue presence floating in a very large, very indifferent neighborhood.

And if Artemis were to continue beyond the Moon, just a little farther, there would come a moment when even that dot disappears. Not destroyed. Not conquered. Just, gone from view.

It’s a humbling thought.

We spend so much time proving how important we are to each other, and so little time remembering how small we are together.

Perhaps the real achievement of Artemis 2 is not that we are returning to the Moon.
It’s that, for a brief moment, we were forced to look back, and see ourselves clearly.

A blue dot.

Still arguing.

📌 Mind-blowing Earth views from Orion on Day 2 of #ArtemisII! 🛰️🌍
These stunning high-resolution photos, taken straight through the capsule window, capture our planet’s gentle curvature, vibrant continents, swirling clouds, and that razor-thin blue line of atmosphere hugging everything we know and love — all against the infinite black of space. 🌌😱
It’s the first time human eyes have seen Earth like this from deep space since the Apollo missions more than 50 years ago. 🤯
A humbling, breathtaking reminder of just how small, fragile, and incredibly unique our home really is. 🥹
Massive credit to the whole international team — and extra pride for Europe: 🥳👇
ESA’s European Service Module is the powerhouse behind Orion, delivering propulsion, electricity from its solar arrays, air, water, and thermal control that keep the four astronauts safe on this historic crewed journey around the Moon. 🛰️🌑
What an unforgettable sight! 👀🚀 
Which part of these images hits you hardest — the glowing blue oceans, the swirling weather systems, or that delicate atmosphere? 🌊🌦️🌪️

Notes on key comparisons (based on official NASA & SpaceX data as of April 2026):
Cost: SLS is government-developed and far more expensive per flight. Starship’s goal is dramatic cost reduction through full reusability.ad2abe
Reusability: SLS is single-use (boosters and engines are discarded). Starship is engineered for rapid full reuse (booster catch + ship landing), with full reusability targeted for 2026 operations.6cfa87
Size: Starship is significantly taller and wider, enabling massive payload capacity (100–150+ tonnes to LEO reusable vs. SLS ~95 tonnes expendable).
Thrust: Starship produces roughly double the liftoff thrust, making it the most powerful rocket ever built.
Top speed: These are mission-dependent. Artemis 2’s SLS sends Orion on a translunar trajectory (higher velocity). Starship’s typical Starlink missions are to low-Earth orbit (~7.8 km/s). Both can achieve higher speeds when needed.
Reusable status (added per your request): Explicitly “No” for SLS; “Yes” (design goal, approaching operational) for Starship.
This highlights the core design philosophy difference: SLS prioritizes maximum power for deep-space human missions (at high cost), while Starship emphasizes reusability and low cost for high-cadence Starlink-style operations.

#Artemis2 Crew:  Reid Wiseman (NASA) — Commander. A U.S. Navy test pilot and veteran of a six-month ISS mission (Expedition 40 in 2014). He previously served as NASA's chief astronaut. Wiseman leads the mission and has described it as a lifelong dream tied to childhood wonder about the Moon.
Victor Glover (NASA) — Pilot. A veteran astronaut (ISS Expedition 64) and U.S. Navy pilot. He will be the first Black astronaut to travel beyond low Earth orbit.
Christina Koch (NASA) — Mission Specialist. An engineer and physicist who holds the record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman (328 days on the ISS). She will be the first woman to fly beyond low Earth orbit. Koch has completed multiple spacewalks.
Jeremy Hansen (Canadian Space Agency — CSA) — Mission Specialist. A former Royal Canadian Air Force fighter pilot and physicist. This is his first spaceflight, making him the first Canadian (and first non-American) to travel to the Moon's vicinity.

No, it’s not a balloon.
The video in that X post (and the still images it shows) is of the actual Artemis 2 SLS rocket sitting on the launch pad at Kennedy Space Center right before launch. The big orange wrinkly “balloon-like” things wrapped around the two huge side boosters are protective weather/thermal covers (sometimes called SRB covers or booster protection blankets).
Why NASA puts them on:
•  The solid rocket boosters (SRBs) are extremely sensitive to moisture, temperature swings, UV rays, salt air, birds, insects, and debris while the rocket sits stacked on the pad for weeks or months.
•  These are heavy-duty fabric/insulating covers that zip or strap on like a giant jacket. They keep the propellant dry and stable.
•  They’re bright orange so crews can easily spot any damage or issues during inspections.
•  They’re not inflated with air or helium — they’re just draped and secured fabric. That’s why they look wrinkly and saggy in photos, exactly like a deflated balloon or a big tarp.
During the countdown and launch, the covers are either manually removed, designed to fall away, or burn off harmlessly when the engines ignite. You can see similar covers used on Artemis 1 and on other SLS tests.
The rest of the video is classic conspiracy editing: it jumps to unrelated clips of contrails, power lines, and a guy in a plane to try to claim “it’s fake / it’s a balloon / we didn’t go to space.” That’s just the usual flat-Earth / “space is gayeee” content the account posts. The rocket itself is 100% real hardware.
So the short answer: it’s a protective cover, not a balloon. NASA isn’t launching a giant party balloon to the Moon — they’re just keeping the real rocket safe until liftoff.

Seeing a lot of tweets that show people don’t realize everything Artemis 2 is doing, including calculating slingshot trajectories with no margin for error, was already done in 1968 and repeated multiple times afterward, including in 1970 when they had to recalculate and execute a new trajectory 150,000 miles from Earth with engines that weren’t designed for it because part of the spacecraft had exploded. All of this was done with computers that had 100,000 times less memory and 1,000 times less processing power than an iPhone. It’s great that it’s being done again, but the real mind blowing thing is that the exact same thing was done 58 years ago with far less technology.
